title: Introduction
text: You've been using Cypher projections throughout this course. Let's take a moment to recall what a Cypher projection looks like. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-3-native-projection-s387-001-new] As you can see here, we have a MATCH statement that connects Movies to Genres, and then we project this graph using `gds.graph.project`. This returns the graph name and node count.

title: Introduction
text: Now, the Python client offers the same capabilities, but instead of using Cypher syntax, you'll use Python dictionaries. This lesson will guide you through translating your Cypher projection knowledge into Python. It's all about using the right tools for the job!

title: What You'll Learn
text: By the end of this lesson, you'll be able to translate Cypher projections into Python syntax, configure node and relationship properties in projections, set relationship orientation—whether undirected or reversed—handle missing properties with default values, and choose between `gds.graph.project()` and `gds.graph.cypher.project()`.

title: The Same Concepts, Different Syntax
text: Both Cypher and Python approaches achieve the same goals: selecting nodes by label, selecting relationships by type, configuring properties and orientation, and creating an in-memory projection. The Python client translates your dictionary configuration into the equivalent Cypher projection. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-3-native-projection-s387-001-new] Notice how this diagram shows the similarities between the two methods, highlighting node selection, relationship selection, and property configuration.

title: Basic Projection: Cypher
text: Here's a quick reminder of how a basic Cypher projection looks. We use a MATCH statement to connect Movies to Genres, and then project this graph. It's straightforward and effective.

title: Basic Projection: Python
text: Now, let's see the Python equivalent. We use `gds.graph.project`, specifying the graph name, node labels, and relationship type. It's a simple translation from Cypher to Python, using lists and strings.

title: Basic Projection: The Pattern
text: The MATCH pattern `(source:Movie)-[r:IN_GENRE]->(target:Genre)` in Cypher becomes two arguments in Python: the labels `['Movie', 'Genre']` and the type `'IN_GENRE'`. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-3-native-projection-s390-002-new] This diagram illustrates how the Cypher MATCH pattern translates into Python arguments, mapping nodes and relationships from Cypher to Python syntax.

title: What gds.graph.project() Returns
text: When you call `gds.graph.project()`, it returns two values: a Graph object and projection metadata. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-3-native-projection-s391-003-new] This flowchart shows the process of calling the function and unpacking its return values into a Graph object and metadata.

title: The Result Metadata
text: The metadata returned by `gds.graph.project()` includes useful information like the graph name, node count, and relationship count. You can access these details easily to understand your graph's structure.

title: Discarding Metadata
text: If you're only interested in the Graph object and not the metadata, you can use an underscore `_` to discard the metadata. This keeps your code clean and focused on what you need.

title: Multiple Labels and Types: Cypher
text: In Cypher, you can handle multiple labels and types by using a MATCH statement with OR conditions and multiple relationship types. This allows for more complex graph structures.

title: Multiple Labels and Types: Python
text: In Python, you achieve the same result by listing multiple labels and relationship types in the `gds.graph.project()` function. It's a flexible way to handle complex graph structures.

title: Adding Node Properties: Cypher
text: In Cypher, you can specify node properties directly within the projection. This allows you to include specific attributes like birth year or IMDb rating in your graph.

title: Adding Node Properties: Python
text: In Python, when you need node properties, labels become dictionary keys with a `properties` list. This lets you specify exactly which attributes to include in your graph projection.

title: Relationship Properties: Cypher
text: Cypher allows you to include relationship properties in your projections. This is useful for capturing details like ratings or weights that are associated with relationships.

title: Relationship Properties: Python
text: In Python, the pattern is similar: when you need configuration, the relationship type becomes a dictionary key. This allows you to include specific properties in your graph projection.

title: Undirected Relationships: Cypher
text: In Cypher, you can specify undirected relationships in your projections. This treats the relationships as bidirectional, which can be useful for certain types of analyses.

title: Undirected Relationships: Python
text: In Python, you achieve undirected relationships by setting the `orientation` to `UNDIRECTED`. This makes it easy to treat relationships as bidirectional in your graph projections.

title: Orientation Options
text: You have several options for relationship orientation: `NATURAL` to keep the original direction, `REVERSE` to flip all directions, and `UNDIRECTED` to treat them as bidirectional. These options give you flexibility in how you model your graph.

title: Default Values for Missing Properties
text: Sometimes, nodes might be missing properties. You can use `defaultValue` to handle these nulls gracefully. This ensures your graph remains robust even when data is incomplete.

title: Default Values
text: Nodes missing a property will use the default value instead of causing an error. This mirrors the `coalesce()` function you used in Cypher projections, providing a safety net for missing data.

title: Monopartite Transformations: Cypher
text: Creating an Actor-to-Actor network through shared Movies requires a complex MATCH pattern in Cypher. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-3-native-projection-s405-006-new] This flowchart depicts the process, showing how actors are connected through shared movies.

title: Monopartite Transformations: Python
text: The `gds.graph.project()` method handles direct label/type projections, but for pattern-based transformations, you'll use `gds.graph.cypher.project()`. This allows you to leverage complex Cypher patterns in your Python code.

title: Alternative: run_cypher + graph.get()
text: You can also run a Cypher projection directly and retrieve the Graph object afterwards. This approach uses the exact Cypher syntax you're already familiar with, making it a seamless transition.

title: Retrieving the Graph Object
text: Once you've run your Cypher projection, you can retrieve the Graph object using `gds.graph.get()`. This lets you access the graph's properties and continue your analysis with ease.

title: Full Movies Graph Projection
text: Here's how you can create a complete projection with all labels and types in Python. This example includes default values for missing properties, ensuring a comprehensive graph structure.

title: When to Use Each Method
text: Choosing the right method depends on your needs. Use `gds.graph.project()` for direct label/type projections, `gds.graph.cypher.project()` for complex patterns, and `run_cypher()` with `graph.get()` if you prefer writing raw Cypher.

title: When to Use Each Method
text: Most projections will work with `gds.graph.project()`. However, when you need pattern transformations like monopartite projections, the Cypher-based methods are your go-to tools.

title: Translation Reference
text: Here's a handy reference for translating Cypher constructs into Python syntax. [IMAGE: img-3-python-client-3-native-projection-s412-008-new] This comparison diagram shows how node labels, properties, and relationship types map from Cypher to Python, highlighting the differences and similarities.

title: Summary
text: In summary, translating Cypher projections to Python involves using strings or lists for simple cases, dictionaries for properties or configuration, and choosing the right method for your projection needs. Use `defaultValue` to handle missing properties. Next, we'll explore running algorithms with the Python client.
